title: Dust
description: A book about inhabiting spaces that no longer hold you. Seven rooms, seven years, photographed after leaving.

sections.title: Prologue
sections.body: She moved through the house the way you move through memory — slowly, not quite touching things. Rooms that held years inside their walls. A window that let in the same light it always had.

sections.title: I. Threshold
sections.body: The door had been left open for so long that birds began treating the hallway as sky. This is what happens when you leave things: they become homes for other things. She stood at the edge of it. Both directions felt like leaving.

sections.title: II. Interior
sections.body: Every object carries a shadow of use. The cup that held morning. The chair that held waiting. To photograph a room is to make an argument — that this particular arrangement of things mattered once, briefly, completely.

sections.title: III. Light Source
sections.body: At 4pm the light came through the west window and made a rectangle on the floor she had been stepping around for thirty years. She finally stepped into it. Stood there until it moved.

sections.title: Coda
sections.body: What we keep is not what we remember. What we photograph is not what we saw. The book you hold is a room you cannot enter — only imagine having left.

title: 54 Rooms
description: A photographic census of rental rooms occupied across three cities over a decade. No people, only evidence of living.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body: Each room photographed the morning after the last night. The bed unmade, the kitchen empty, the window showing whatever city this was.

sections.title: Delhi, 2013–2016
sections.body: The rooms in Lajpat Nagar all had the same yellow ceiling. I thought it was the building until I moved and found the same yellow in Karol Bagh. It was the light.

sections.title: Mumbai, 2016–2020
sections.body: In Mumbai the rooms are smaller but the windows are larger. You trade space for sky. This seems like a bad deal until you have lived without the sky for long enough.

sections.title: Bengaluru, 2020–2023
sections.body: The pandemic rooms are the ones I cannot look at for long. They have a quality of arrested time that the others don't. The others feel like departures. These feel like stays.

title: Border Radio
description: A 40-minute video work combining field recordings from the border zone with testimonies of radio operators from 1971.

sections.title: Artist statement
sections.body: The static between stations as a form of mourning. The voices that come through as a form of archive. I spent two years recording along the border — not at the checkpoints but in the fields, the tea shops, the radio rooms.

sections.title: On the work
sections.body: What field recording teaches you is that silence is never empty. Border Radio tries to make audible what was never meant to be preserved — the ambient sound of a line that should not exist.

title: Monsoon Journal
description: A photo diary spanning seven monsoon seasons in North Kolkata — same streets, different light, different floods.

sections.title: Season one, 2017
sections.body: Rain changes what the street is willing to show you. The first monsoon I photographed in North Kolkata I understood almost nothing of what I was seeing.

sections.title: Season four, 2020
sections.body: By the fourth year I had stopped trying to make pictures. I walked and let the rain decide. What came out of the camera surprised me — not because it was unexpected but because it was true.

sections.title: Season seven, 2023
sections.body: The same street for seven years. The wall that was repainted three times. The chai stall that moved, came back, moved again. Rain as the only reliable thing.

title: The Quiet Industry
description: A photographic essay on the small workshops of Howrah — foundries, lathes, metal dust — made over three years.

sections.title: Preface
sections.body: Industry at this scale has no spectacle. Only repetition, only the smell of metal, only hands. I came to photograph Howrah because someone told me it was dying. Three years later I think they were wrong.

title: A Long Season
description: Tracing seasonal change across a single field in Punjab over 18 months. A study in duration and attention.

sections.title: Note
sections.body: The work refuses a single vantage point. I returned to the same field every three weeks for eighteen months, always arriving at different times of day. The field is the same field. The light is never the same light.
